does: This paper characterizes a fundamental tradeoff in sparse autoencoders (SAEs) between three competing objectives: reconstruction accuracy (low distortion), efficient feature usage (low rate), and interpretability (monosemanticity). The authors prove that forcing SAEs to learn monosemantic features—where each neuron responds to a single semantic concept—necessarily increases both the number of features required and reconstruction error. Under a generative modeling framework, they show that optimal polysemanticity (neurons responding to multiple concepts) is determined by the input distribution, particularly feature co-occurrence probabilities. They derive necessary conditions for polysemanticity measures when the data-generating process is unknown and benchmark existing metrics against synthetic and real data.
connects: This work extends rate-distortion theory, a classical framework from information theory, into the mechanistic interpretability domain by formalizing tensions that practitioners have observed in SAE design. It builds on recent advances in using SAEs for neural network interpretation, particularly work showing that purely monosemantic representations sacrifice reconstruction quality or require excessive hidden dimensions. The paper connects to the broader literature on feature learning and interpretability that has questioned whether perfect interpretability is always achievable or desirable, and relates to information-theoretic analyses of representation learning that examine what can be recovered from compressed representations.
enables: This work provides a principled foundation for designing SAEs that make conscious tradeoffs among interpretability, efficiency, and fidelity rather than optimizing each independently. It enables researchers to predict the polysemanticity patterns that will emerge for different data distributions, informing choices about SAE architecture and training objectives. The theoretical framework could guide the development of new polysemanticity metrics better suited to mechanistic interpretability applications. More broadly, the rate-distortion-polysemanticity perspective could inspire new SAE training objectives that explicitly balance these three dimensions, potentially enabling tools that sacrifice some interpretability for substantially improved reconstruction or feature efficiency when appropriate for downstream analysis tasks.
